items.question_text: Complete the traffic management mission statement: the system's mission is to _____ air traffic _____ with system capacity to ensure the maximum efficient utilization of the NAS.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1a, the mission is to balance air traffic demand with system capacity to ensure maximum efficient utilization of the NAS.

choices.text: balance; demand
balance, demand

items.question_text: When both TBFM and miles-in-trail initiatives are available as flow management tools, which takes priority and what is the required threshold for that preference?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1b, TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to miles-in-trail initiatives. The standard requires maximum feasible use, not merely equal-standing preference.

choices.text: TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT.
TBFM and MIT have equal standing; the controller selects whichever is easier for the current sector.
MIT must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to TBFM.
TBFM is preferred only when MIT would require coordination with more than two adjacent facilities.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-1, TBFM may be set aside in favor of miles-in-trail initiatives whenever doing so would simplify sector operations for the current shift.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1b, TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT. Convenience or shift-level simplicity alone does not override that requirement; TBFM is only set aside when it is not feasible.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the STMCIC operational briefing requirement: the briefing must be conducted at least once during the _____ and _____ shifts.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a1, the STMCIC must ensure an operational briefing is conducted at least once during the day and evening shifts.

choices.text: day, evening
day and evening

items.question_text: When departure scheduling or Call for Release is in effect, ATCTs must release aircraft so they are airborne within a window that extends from _____ minutes prior and ends _____ minute(s) after the assigned time, unless otherwise coordinated.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2f3, the window extends from 2 minutes prior and ends 1 minute after the assigned time, unless otherwise coordinated.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, what must the STMCIC do regarding TMC duties when the TMC position is not staffed?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a2, the STMCIC must assume responsibility for TMC duties when not staffed. This duty belongs to the STMCIC specifically, not the OS/CIC or controllers.

choices.text: Assume responsibility for TMC duties.
Transfer TMC duties to the OS/CIC for the duration of the vacancy.
Assign TMC duties to any available controller regardless of qualification.
Suspend all TMC functions until a TMC reports for duty.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, what is the OS/CIC required to do regarding TBM operations and TMIs affecting their area of responsibility?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2b3, the OS/CIC must continuously review TBM operations and TMIs affecting their area and coordinate with TMU for extensions, revisions, or cancellations.

choices.text: Continuously review TBM operations and TMIs affecting their area, and coordinate with TMU for extensions, revisions, or cancellations.
Accept whatever TMIs are in place and only coordinate changes at shift change.
Review TMIs only when a controller reports a specific problem with metering compliance.
Coordinate TMIs exclusively with adjacent facilities, not with the TMU.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, which duty is assigned to the STMCIC but NOT directly assigned to the OS/CIC?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2, the STMCIC must ensure an operational briefing is conducted (a1) and must assume TMC duties when not staffed (a2). The OS/CIC duties focus on keeping parties apprised, coordinating TBM/TMIs, continuously reviewing, ensuring enforcement, EDST entries, outage actions, and timely implementation of changes. While both roles share some duties, the briefing and TMC-assumption duties are STMCIC-specific.

choices.text: Ensure an operational briefing is conducted and assume responsibility for TMC duties when not staffed.
Keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of congestion-causing situations.
Continuously review TBM operations and TMIs and coordinate changes with the TMU.
Ensure changes to TBM operations and TMIs are implemented in a timely manner.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, which of the following is a requirement for TRACONs that is NOT required of ARTCCs?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2, ARTCCs must support TBFM operations, monitor TBFM equipment, and monitor arrival flow (d1-d2). TRACONs must do all of that (e1-e2) plus additionally schedule internal departures per specific written procedures (e3).

choices.text: Schedule internal departures in accordance with specific written procedures and agreements developed with overlying ARTCCs and adjacent facilities.
Support TBFM operations and monitor TBFM equipment for situational awareness.
Monitor arrival flow for potential metering actions or changes and initiate coordination if needed.
Perform assigned actions in the event of an EDST outage or degradation.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, when departure scheduling is in effect and an ATCT is equipped, how must the ATCT obtain a departure release time?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2f2, when equipped and departure scheduling is in effect, ATCTs must use automation to obtain a departure release time from the TBM system.

choices.text: Use automation to obtain a departure release time from the TBM system.
Call the overlying ARTCC by landline for each individual departure release.
Use any available voice frequency to request a release, regardless of existing agreements.
Coordinate departure times only with the TMU through manual message entry.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, coordination between facilities regarding TBM operations and TMIs may only be accomplished through written documentation.
items.explanation: Per the note at 11-1-2, coordination may be verbal, electronic, or written. Written documentation is not the only acceptable method.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, what do TBM operations and TMIs not have priority over for personnel providing air traffic services?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2c1, TBM operations and TMIs do not have priority over maintaining separation of aircraft and procedural integrity of the sector.

choices.text: Separation of aircraft and procedural integrity of the sector.
Fuel efficiency and pilot-preferred routing.
On-time performance metrics and published AAR targets.
Expeditious handling of military aircraft in all circumstances.

items.question_text: During periods of metering, personnel providing air traffic services must display TBFM schedule information on the _____.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3a, during periods of metering, personnel must display TBFM schedule information on the situation display.

choices.text: situation display

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, what should personnel do when TBFM-generated metering time accuracy cannot be used for specific aircraft because of significant jumps in the delay countdown timer?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b1, when DCT instability prevents accurate metering-time compliance, TMIs such as miles-in-trail or minutes-in-trail may be used between the affected aircraft to assist in delay absorption until stability resumes.

choices.text: Use TMIs such as miles-in-trail or minutes-in-trail between those aircraft to assist delay absorption until stability resumes.
Ignore the metering times entirely for all aircraft in the sector until the end of the shift.
Discontinue TBFM display and coordinate only after stability has already returned.
Apply the plus-or-minus-1-minute standard to all aircraft regardless of DCT behavior.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, an exception to the plus-or-minus-1-minute metering compliance requirement may be authorized for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b2, an exception may be authorized for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When compliance with TBFM-generated metering times is not possible during metering, with whom must personnel coordinate under 11-1-3?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3c, when compliance is not possible, coordinate with OS/CIC, personnel providing traffic management services, and adjacent facilities/sectors as appropriate.

choices.text: OS/CIC, personnel providing traffic management services, and adjacent facilities or sectors as appropriate.
Only the TMU, because metering compliance is exclusively a traffic management function.
Only the overlying ARTCC, because compliance decisions are made at the center level.
No coordination is required; controllers may independently deviate when metering targets cannot be met.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, TBFM accuracy of generated metering times can be affected by TMU resequencing a specific flight or flight list.
items.explanation: Per the note at 11-1-3, TBFM accuracy is predicated on several factors, including when TMU resequences a specific flight or flight list. Caution is advised in such situations.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Where can complete details of TBM, traffic management initiatives, and programs be found according to 11-1-1?

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.
FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 11, Section 2.
Only in local facility directives developed by each ARTCC.
The Aeronautical Information Manual, Chapter 4.

items.explanation: Per the note at 11-1-1, complete details of TBM, traffic management initiatives and programs can be found in FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.

items.situation: An STMCIC is planning to use miles-in-trail restrictions as the primary flow strategy during a period of moderate demand. TBFM tools are operational and available. When asked why TBFM isn't being used first, the STMCIC says MIT is simpler and has fewer training requirements.
items.question_text: How should the traffic management tool selection be handled?

choices.text: Use TBFM to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT, because the order requires TBFM preference when feasible.
Continue with MIT because the STMCIC has discretion to choose whichever tool is more convenient.
Use TBFM only if all controllers on position request it, since controller preference overrides the order.
Use MIT during this shift and defer TBFM adoption until the next order revision.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-1b, TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT. Simplicity or training preference does not override the required preference for TBFM when it is available and operational.

items.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a topic that the STMCIC operational briefing discussion should include?

choices.text: Pilot currency requirements and individual controller training schedules.
Meteorological conditions, both present and forecasted.
Staffing, equipment status, runways in use, and Airport Arrival Rate.
TBM use and Traffic Management Initiatives, present and anticipated.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a1, the briefing should include meteorological conditions, staffing, equipment status, runways in use, AAR, TBM use, and TMIs. Pilot currency requirements and individual training schedules are not listed among the briefing topics.

items.situation: During an evening shift, a controller notices that an active TMI in their area of responsibility is creating excessive delay because weather conditions have significantly improved since the TMI was implemented. The controller considers waiting until the end of the shift to address it.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle the TMI under these changed conditions?

choices.text: Continuously review the TMI affecting their area and coordinate with OS/CIC and TMU for extensions, revisions, or cancellations as appropriate.
Wait until the end of the shift, because TMIs are only reviewed at shift-change briefings.
Unilaterally discontinue the TMI without coordination because conditions have improved.
Treat the TMI as valid for the duration of the metering period regardless of changing conditions.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2c3, personnel providing air traffic services must continuously review TBM operations and TMIs affecting their area and coordinate with OS/CIC and TMU for extensions, revisions, or cancellations.

items.situation: A supervisor notices that a recently implemented TMI has not been effectively communicated to the affected sectors. One sector is applying it inconsistently and another is unaware it exists. The OS/CIC had been busy with other coordination and missed the notification.
items.question_text: What should the OS/CIC have done to prevent this situation?

choices.text: Keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of situations or circumstances that may cause congestion or delays, including current TMIs.
Defer all TMI notifications to the STMCIC, because only the STMCIC communicates with sectors.
Post the TMI on a bulletin board, because written notification alone satisfies the requirement.
Wait for controllers to discover the TMI through automation and ask questions when confused.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2b1, the OS/CIC must keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of situations or circumstances that may cause congestion or delays. Active TMIs that are not communicated undermine the traffic management mission.

items.question_text: During periods of metering, what are the two foundational TBFM compliance steps that personnel providing air traffic services must take?

choices.text: Display TBFM schedule information on the situation display and comply with generated metering times within plus or minus 1 minute.
Display TBFM schedule information on the supervisor station and comply within plus or minus 3 minutes.
Display TBFM schedule information on paper strips and comply within plus or minus 5 miles of the metering fix.
Display TBFM schedule information only when TMU requests it, with no defined compliance tolerance.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-3a-b, the foundational requirements are to display TBFM schedule information on the situation display and comply with generated metering times within plus or minus 1 minute.

items.situation: During an active metering period, a controller cannot meet the plus-or-minus-1-minute compliance standard for several aircraft due to an unexpected traffic complexity spike. The controller considers doing their best without notifying anyone, reasoning that the deviation is temporary.
items.question_text: What is the required action when metering-time compliance is not possible?

choices.text: Coordinate with OS/CIC, personnel providing traffic management services, and adjacent facilities or sectors as appropriate.
Make a best-effort attempt and document the deviation only after the shift ends.
Suspend all metering operations in the sector until the complexity subsides.
Coordinate only with adjacent sectors, because TMU and OS/CIC are not required parties for non-compliance.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-3c, when compliance is not possible, personnel must coordinate with OS/CIC, personnel providing traffic management services, and adjacent facilities or sectors as appropriate.
